facts_disclosed: Hauls flatbed — construction materials and steel

turns.content: man my shoulders are done like completely done i cant even lift my arms right now
That sounds rough! What happened — did you overdo it at the gym or something?

nah man its work i do flatbed loads right so today i had steel beams going from a yard outside birmingham up to a job site in chattanooga and you gotta strap all that down yourself like nobody helps you its just you and the load and the straps and the tarps
Oh man, flatbed work is serious. Steel beams are no joke either — those are heavy loads to secure. How many straps does something like that usually take?

depends on the length but today it was like 14 straps and 6 chains because steel you gotta chain down you cant just strap it and the whole time its raining and its january so my hands are numb and the tarps are wet and heavy and i was listening to this podcast about stoicism and marcus aurelius was like the obstacle is the way and i was thinking marcus aurelius never tarped a flatbed at 5am in february weather in alabama

Ha! I'm pretty sure Marcus Aurelius would have had some revised philosophies if he'd had to tarp steel beams in freezing rain. That's a brutal start to the day.

Do you always haul steel, or do you get different types of loads?

its mostly construction materials you know steel beams lumber concrete forms whatever needs to go to a job site somewhere and the construction guys they dont care if its raining or its 30 degrees they need their stuff on site by 7am so you gotta be there early and have it all secured and tarped right because if one strap comes loose on the highway thats on you thats your cdl thats your livelihood you know

That's a lot of responsibility riding on your shoulders — literally. A loose strap on the highway with steel beams is a nightmare scenario. Do you ever get loads that are easier to deal with, or is it pretty much always the heavy construction stuff?

sometimes i get lumber which is easier because at least its lighter to work with but the worst is when you get mixed loads like steel and lumber on the same trailer because they shift different and you gotta secure them separate and the whole thing takes like 2 hours to strap down properly and by the end your back is shot your shoulders are burning and you havent even started driving yet
Two hours of physical labor before you even turn the key — that's a whole workout before work. And then you have to drive for how many hours after that?

today it was only like 4 hours of driving which is short for me but the loading and unloading at both ends thats another story the guys at the job site in chattanooga had a crane to unload the steel so that was fine but sometimes you show up and they got nothing no forklift no crane and they looking at you like can you help us unload and im like brother i drove this 300 miles i am not unloading it too sa k pase thats not in my job description
Right! You're the driver, not the unloading crew. That's a whole separate job. Do they usually have proper equipment at the construction sites, or is it hit or miss?

hit or miss for real like the big sites always have equipment but the smaller jobs sometimes its just three guys and a prayer you know and i feel bad but also mwen fatige like i already did my part getting it there safely on a flatbed in the rain i cant be lifting steel beams by hand too im 35 not 25 anymore

Absolutely — your body can only take so much, especially with all the tarping and strapping you already do. At 35, you've got to protect your back and joints. Is the wear and tear on your body something you think about a lot with this kind of work?

yeah i mean flatbed is the hardest type of trucking physically like dry van guys they just back up to a dock and someone else loads it but flatbed you are out there in the elements wrestling tarps and chains and its brutal in winter but it pays a little more because nobody wants to do it and i kinda take pride in it you know like not everybody can do this work its a skill securing a load right so it goes 70 mph down the interstate and nothing moves

That's a real craft — keeping everything locked down tight at highway speeds. There's a reason flatbed pays more: it's harder, more physical, more skill-intensive, and the stakes are higher. You should take pride in that.

Have you thought about switching to dry van or something less physical eventually, or do you prefer the flatbed life despite the beating it gives you?

i think about it sometimes especially on mornings like today when im out there in the cold and the rain but then i get on the road and the load is secure and im rolling and i got my podcast going and i dont know man theres something about flatbed that feels more real like youre actually doing something you know not just pulling a box

I get that — there's a tangible satisfaction in flatbed work that you don't get with a sealed trailer. You can see the load, you secured it yourself, and you know exactly what's riding behind you. That sense of craftsmanship matters.

What's your next load looking like? More steel, or something different?

tomorrow i got a lumber run going down to a site near macon so at least thats lighter and the weather is supposed to be dry so no wrestling wet tarps thank god anyway im at a pilot right now outside chattanooga about to get some food and crash for the night bondye bon i survived another day of tarping

Ha! Bondye bon indeed — you earned that rest. Dry weather and lumber sounds like a much kinder day ahead. Go get some food and let those shoulders recover. Flatbed life isn't for everybody, but it sounds like it's in your blood even when it's beating you up. Get some sleep.
